reference_answer: Idris Pritchard, in 1948

Ten named trees or tree units: King Pear, Duchess Apple, Cider Row (three trees as one unit), Blenheim Orange, Quince Pair (two trees as one unit), Damson Wall, Medlar, Cherry Arch, Worcester Pearmain, and Greengage
King Pear, Duchess Apple, Cider Row, Blenheim Orange, Quince Pair, and Damson Wall (the western half)
Medlar, Cherry Arch, Worcester Pearmain, and Greengage (the eastern half)
Because the eastern half contained fewer trees than the western half
No. The court notes it was never made and the creditor estate has been fully distributed.
A £4,000 loan from a local agricultural cooperative in 1997
No. The debt was satisfied in 2007 (acknowledged by the cooperative) and the formal release was executed in March 2009.
2007, when the cooperative acknowledged the debt as satisfied after three harvest seasons totaling £1,350 against the £1,200 remaining debt
March 2009
Douglas Pye, Bronwen's neighbor
No. Bronwen explicitly described it as 'a loan of harvest rights, not a transfer of ownership,' confirmed in a 2003 letter to her solicitor.

The solicitor advised that the verbal harvest loan likely terminated on Bronwen's death since it was not a formal easement. However, this has not been adjudicated. Pye continued harvesting regardless.
Eighteen years (2001 to 2019)
No. The probate court notes that Pye has not filed a formal adverse possession claim.
Her nephew Rhodri, Gethin's son
All twelve trees (ten named units): six from Gethin's western half and four from Bronwen's eastern half
His daughter Ffion
A birthday card on which Rhodri wrote 'The Greengage is yours, my darling. Happy birthday.' Plus Carys has maintained and harvested the tree since 2013.
No.
No. The court deferred the matter to a full hearing. It has not yet ruled on whether the card constitutes a valid gift or whether maintenance establishes an equitable claim.
Carys, since 2013
Wye Valley Pressings, since 2016
Yes. It remains physically in the orchard under an access agreement for annual harvest.
Rhodri sold it to Wye Valley Pressings in 2016 for £800 with a proper bill of sale.
No. The probate court confirms it was validly sold.
King Pear, Duchess Apple, Quince Pair, Damson Wall, Cherry Arch, and Worcester Pearmain
Ffion, provisionally, pending the Greengage hearing and any potential Medlar claim by Pye
Disputed. Carys claims it was gifted in 2013. The probate court has deferred the question to a full hearing.
Noted but not adjudicated. Pye has not filed a formal claim. The court observes his continuous harvest may support an adverse possession claim if filed, but no determination has been made.
Ffion (uncontested inheritance)
Ffion (the pledge was satisfied and released; the Cider Row is part of the estate)
Douglas Pye has been harvesting it continuously, most recently through 2024
Carys, since 2013
No. The document explicitly distinguishes between possession (physical harvest/maintenance), ownership (legal title), inheritance, pledging, and borrowing as different states.

That the verbal harvest loan likely terminated on Bronwen's death since it was not a formal easement, but that Pye could argue adverse possession if he had harvested continuously and openly for twelve or more years
It is noted but not adjudicated. The court observes Pye's continuous harvest may support an adverse possession claim if filed, but makes no determination.
Approximately £1,350 (approximately £450 per season over three seasons: 2005, 2006, 2007)
Yes. The outstanding debt was £1,200 and the cooperative harvested approximately £1,350, an over-recovery of approximately £150.

The Greengage (disputed gift, deferred to full hearing), the Medlar (Pye's harvest rights and potential adverse possession claim, not adjudicated), and the £600 equalization payment (historical debt, creditor estate fully distributed, no action taken)
